content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 27, 1989

Rozellah Ryan
Senior Staff Counsel
Legal Affairs Division
Department of Corrections
1515 S Street, Room 300S
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-89-683

Dear Ms. Ryan:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice in con-
nection with your duty to advise the State Department of Correc-
tions concerning the department's duties under the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1/  You have requested advice with respect
to a Department of Corrections employee who simultaneously serves
on the board of directors of a political committee. Because your
situation does not involve a governmental decision, we can only
provide this general explanation of the requirements of the Act.2/

QUESTION

     Does the Act prohibit a State Civil Service Employee from
also serving on the board of directors of a political committee?

CONCLUSION

     The Act does not prohibit a State Civil Service Employee from
serving on the board of directors of a political committee.
However, you may want to consult your agency's statement of

_______________________

1/   Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/   Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

File No. I-89-683
Page 2

incompatible activities to determine if this activity is permitted
by your agency.

DISCUSSION

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. The Act does not prohibit a public official
from holding public office due to financial interests he or she
may hold. Thus, a State Civil Service Employee may serve on the
board of directors of a political committee and retain his public
position whether the committee is a financial interest of the of-
ficial or not. (Cove Advice Letter, No. A-81-067, copy enclosed.)

However, if the committee becomes a financial interest of the
official, as defined in Section 87103, and it is reasonably
foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on the committee, the official will be obligated
to disqualify him or herself from participating in that decision.
For future reference we have enclosed a pamphlet entitled
"California's Conflict of Interest Laws for Public Officials"
which summarizes the Act's conflict of interest laws. Please
note, however, that the information we are providing is limited to
the Political Reform Act. Other laws may apply to the situation
you have posed, such as the general doctrine of incompatible of-
fices or the Department of Correction's list of incompatibility
activities established pursuant to Government Code Section 19990.
Since the Commission's jurisdiction extends only to matters
covered by the Act, we cannot provide you with assistance
regarding these provisions of law. (Section 83111.)

If you have any further questions concerning this matter or
questions concerning specific decisions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JWW:plh

Enclosure

/8942C

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 9581-

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Inte
(916) 322 5662 322 5660 322 5901 322 6441 322 6444

October 9, 1981

MR. Peter Coye
777 West Seventh Street
Claremont, CA 91711

Dear Mr. Coye:

I am responding to your letter to Mr. Prim of October 2,
1981. You asked whether the Political Reform Act in any way
prohibits you from working for a political committee called
Californians for a Fair Index on time outside your forty
hour work week as an Administrative Assistant for Senator
Watson. I would first like to point out that the Political
Reform Act does not prohibit any political activities.
However, the Act does require that certain activities be
reported. In particular, the Act requires that political
activities by a government employee during government working
hours or with government Facilities or materials, be reported
as an in kind contribution by the government employer. See
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18420, a copy of which is enclosed
for your convenience.

According to your letter, you will not be doing any
work for Californians for a Fair Index during your forty
hour work week for the state. In this case, assuming that
no state facilities or materials are used for Californians
for a Fair Index, your activities would not be considered a
contribution by the state to Californians for a Fair Index,
and would not have to be reported under Section 18420.

I hope this answers your question. If you have any
other questions concerning reporting of campaign expenditures
under the Political Reform Act, please feel free to get in
touch with us.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:km
Enclosure

State of California

Memorandum

Date : November 20, 1989

To : JOHN LARSON
    Chairman
    Fair Political Practices Commission

From : Department of Corrections

Subject : STATE CIVIL SERVICE EMPLOYEE'S NOMINATION TO POLITICAL ACTIVITY COMMITTEE
         Legal Log No. 89-724


The purpose of this memo is to learn whether the Fair Political Activity
Committee has an opinion on the matter of a State of California civil servant
serving on the board of directors for a political activity committee. A
Department of Corrections parole agent has requested permission to serve on
the board of directors for a political activity committee in Contra Costa
County. He reports that the sole function of the board is to decide on the
disbursement of funds to support candidates running for election. A State
Assemblyman is nominating the employee.

If you have any information which could assist the Department in handling
this employee's request, it would be appreciated.


ROZELLAH RYAN
Senior Staff Counsel
Legal Affairs Division

December 1, 1989


Rozellah Ryan
Department of Corrections
Legal Affairs Division
1515 S Street, Room 300S
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                          Re:  Letter No. 89-683

Dear Ms. Ryan:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on November 27, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact John Wallace an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

                                          Very truly yours,


                                          [illegible]

                                          Kathryn E. Donovan
                                          General Counsel
